nativeRange.habitat: Coastal and inner-reef areas with rubble, soft bottoms, or flat substrates featuring rock, coral, sponge, and seawhip outcrops; generally encountered between about 5 and 20 m depth.

diet.notes: Generally advised to offer a varied diet that includes Spirulina, nori and other marine algae, mysis shrimp, krill, chopped clams, and angelfish preparations containing sponge material. In the wild, this species has been documented feeding heavily on sponges and tunicates, so sponge-enriched foods tend to support long-term health. Frequent small feedings, especially during the first weeks after introduction, are commonly recommended.

summary: An Australian/Indo-West Pacific medium-large angelfish with striking yellow-and-blue scribbled markings. Generally considered intermediate to care for; tends to nip corals and clam mantles and is commonly considered not reef-safe.
careNotes: The Scribbled Angelfish is generally regarded as one of the more manageable medium-large Chaetodontoplus angels, but it still tends to need an experienced marine keeper. Newly introduced individuals are commonly reported as shy and may refuse food for several days; offering sponge-enriched preparations alongside mysis, nori, and other meaty/algal foods has been documented to ease the transition. Multiple hiding places among live rock plus open swimming space are strongly advised. Like other angelfishes, this species is a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first sex changer), and pairs/harems with one dominant male tend to be the natural social structure; aquarists most commonly keep single specimens because conspecifics and similar-pattern Chaetodontoplus tend to be intolerant of each other in captivity. Males have been reported to show a small white patch on the gill cover and more sinuous blue body lines than females. It is commonly considered not reef-safe — most specimens have been documented nipping at stony and soft corals and clam mantles, although isolated reports of well-behaved individuals exist. Cross-source disagreement on temperature was notable: LiveAquaria states 22–26°C while FishBase reports a wild preference of about 25–29°C; the recorded 24–27°C range is the average across sources. HLLE (head-and-lateral-line erosion) has been reported in poorly maintained large angelfish systems, so stable water quality and a vitamin-rich marine-algae diet are generally advised.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported but remains uncommon and is generally considered advanced. Like all angelfishes, the species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals begin as female and the dominant fish may transition to male. Wild spawning has been documented to produce roughly 5,000–33,000 pelagic eggs of about 0.9 mm; raising the planktonic larvae is reported to be very difficult and requires specialised live-feed culture.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source for aquarium-trade husbandry: 180-gallon minimum, semi-aggressive temperament, not reef-safe, omnivore diet, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, temperature 22–26°C, 10" maximum size.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked max length (28 cm TL), depth range (5–20 m), wild temperature preference (about 24.9–29.3°C, mean ~28.4°C), Indo-West Pacific distribution, family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes. Temperature range disagrees with LiveAquaria (22–26°C); values were averaged to 24–27°C.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (28 cm), minimum tank size (~700 L / 185 gal), diet emphasis on algae plus sponges/tunicates/small crustaceans, conspecific aggression toward similar-pattern Chaetodontoplus, protogynous hermaphroditism, and intermediate care level.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used only for taxonomy and natural-history facts (described 1867 by Günther as Holacanthus duboulayi; protogynous hermaphrodite producing roughly 5,000–33,000 eggs; distribution from Shark Bay WA to Moreton Bay QLD and south to Lord Howe Island, plus Aru Islands, southern New Guinea, and Taiwan; depth 5–20 m). Not used for husbandry parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning has been reported but raising the pelagic larvae is generally considered very difficult and requires specialised live-feed culture.
